speaker: interviewer
text: I need to understand how your family was feeling at that time and what effects were being felt by them of the war that was coming closer.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Now, to start with my brother-in-law from Chemnitz came with my sister and the two boys. It would have been in-- by the end of 1939 or in the middle of '39. I can't tell you exactly. Came from Chemnitz, Germany, to Holland. And he told us what's happening, which we knew about it. You read in the paper, but if you hear it, word by00 mouth to mouth, it sounds different. We were very concerned. But again, we thought Holland is going to be neutral. My sister, Anna, who lived in Antwerp, which is very important, with my brother-in-law, Isaac Feigenbaum. And at that time, Paulette, I think they were also convinced Holland, Belgium, are not going to be touched. We didn't think that Germany is going to attack Poland. We didn't think that at all. And then we heard that Chamberlain story. You know, Chamberlain who made peace with Hitler, which was in 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: He gave first part of Czechoslovakia away, and then later on, the whole of Czechoslovakia. We still were not at war. So we were not convinced that there will be another war. Right? And that was the situation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Our family was listening to the news every day. But even thinking of the many times I went to the synagogue, it wasn't that fear that Hitler is going to come to Holland. We didn't think about it. Next question, please, Shirley.

speaker: interviewer
text: At what point did you feel some effect of the war on your family, on your business, on a change in the township, a change in attitudes by the people towards your family or towards the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say the Dutch people were not anti-Semitic. And maybe they were too placid. We were thinking-- there was always what they call in Dutch, the waterlinie. They can put part of Holland under water if the Germans are coming. But there was never a doubt. It never happened. That we thought that the Germans would be coming.

speaker: interviewer
text: The attitude of the people to us was very, very nice. And when we got into the war time, in May, 1940, I can tell you where we not-- where we were not allowed to listen to the radio. We had neighbors that called us up, and we listened there to Churchill, to England, and everything, to all, all the news, you know? And you realize that if they caught somebody listening to the radio or anything like that, these people, they're getting killed. There was no doubt about it. All right? So-- Tell me about--

speaker: interviewee
text: The attitude-- there was no anti-Semitism at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about May, 1940.

speaker: interviewee
text: May, 1940. The Germans attacked Holland on the 10th of May, 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that anticipated?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Definitely not. And we listened to the radio. And we very, very, worried. And we were still thinking the waterlinie, water line, is going to hold the Germans back. We saw all these planes flying over. We were in a panic. And we did something very stupid. My brother, my father, and myself, on the 15th of May, we left Scheveningen because we thought the Germans are only going for the Jews, for the men.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we left Scheveningen, and we went to a place called [NON-ENGLISH]-- [NON-ENGLISH] is opposite England-- to catch a boat to England. Fortunately, we missed that boat because how could we leave with-- and leave Holland-- but we were in a panic. We didn't know what we were doing. How could we leave Holland and leaving the women by themselves? That was-- it was irresponsible. And up to this minute, I can't understand it. We came back via Rotterdam. Rotterdam was in flames.

speaker: interviewee
text: As you probably know, Rotterdam was bombed, because the Germans had-- only had so many hours to get right through Holland. Which the war in Holland only lasted five days.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we were occupied by the Germans in no time. We saw them marching within a few days, right? And as we all know, they went through Belgium, and they went through France.

speaker: interviewee
text: And for some other reason, they did not attack the English army, which we're in on the way back, which we are in Dunkirk, right, on the way back to England. That was a miracle because that army went back to England to defend England. We did not do that. How we felt in Holland with that occupation, we felt-- we felt very insecure. We still did not realize that they were going to do in Holland what they did in Germany. We knew that in Germany they put outside the coffee houses, outside cinemas, the theaters, placards, sign 'Juden Verboten.' We had this within three months. The coffee houses, the theaters, the picture theaters, they all had that sign.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel when you first saw that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Terrible, terrible, terrible. But you know what happened? The Dutch were nice. Their little coffee houses, they put it on the back door where nobody could see it. Because we went in, in 1940, we went sailing on [NON-ENGLISH]. And we went there to [NON-ENGLISH], which was one of the coffee shops. And that sign wasn't in the front, but it was in the back. We went there. And we felt reasonably secure being on a boat on that little island.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then something happens. Do you want me to go on? Mm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then something happened that was in August, 1940. A German soldier got killed in Scheveningen. And the street was the parkland. And within days, they picked up some of the Jewish boys. So we all went into hiding. I was hiding as from August, September, 1940, until I left Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about the day you went into hiding? What did you take?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I can't tell you. The day after they found the German soldier, it was around, because we-- our-- how would I say-- is not the telephone it was told. We had a telephone. We went from house to house. The Jews warned each other. They're picking up the Dutch boys. They picked up three Dutch-- three Dutch Jewish boys. They never came back. We didn't even know what happened to them. So everybody was in hiding.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you take with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I took-- I, I took with me, you know, something clothes. I took with me some underwear. But during the day, we went back whatever we needed that was all right. You were only there for the night. And I was in hiding by the station master, which again, was the [NON-ENGLISH], close to the-- not the cinema, close to the jail. You know there's a jail in Scheveningen. He lived very well, the stationmaster.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: That I can't think of the name. I can't think of the name. That's impossible. But they were extremely nice to me. They were not well off, but whatever they had, they shared with me. But it speaks by itself that I tried to contribute to something other. They didn't want that. They knew that they were in danger if they got caught having me there. In danger. They would have been killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do all day long?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went back to the business. I stayed in the business as long as possible, until I left Holland during the war time. I think it was in January or February, 1941, that they put a [GERMAN]. Now, to translate, the [GERMAN], a German [GERMAN], his name was Albert [NON-ENGLISH] from Cologne, into our business. [GERMAN] was a man who was a supervisor and who was watching that nothing should disappear. I must say that this Albert [NON-ENGLISH] was not an anti-Semite, because I met him after the war. It got to this stage that he knew, and he might even have-- maybe for personal reasons, I'm not sure about it, but he gave us the green lights, not by saying, disappear, but by indicating it would be better if we would leave Holland. And he knew, possibly not with me, but when my brother left, or when my father left. And when my brother left, he knew they were leaving. And he kept his mouth shut.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, so I told you about what happened in Scheveningen. And we still went to the synagogue. We were afraid. There was not a metal of courage because they were so much stronger. We saw them marching in the street everywhere.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were, at the time-- don't forget, 1940-- they occupied nearly the whole of Europe. And before Holland, it was Norway. It was Denmark before Holland. And we were looking for a way out. To go to England was nearly impossible.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know there were some people which left for England. Yes. But to go to England was impossible. So the only way of getting away, if you want me to start with that, yes?

speaker: interviewee
text: The only way of getting away with that was going from Holland to Belgium. And from Belgium to occupied France. From occupied France to unoccupied France, and either to Switzerland or to Spain. As young as I was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --I decided, I'm going. At that particular time, my girlfriend was Mellita. And as my father was very, very religious, I thought he's going to put his back up that I'm leaving with somebody which I'm not married with.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I decided the date to go would be the 24th of November. Why the 24th of November? Don't ask me that question. I just thought I am leaving on the 24th of November.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before I left I went to see my very close friend, Mennol and Thea Anholt, which are still alive. And I told them that I'm leaving tomorrow, which was dangerous to tell anybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: I went to another friend, and that is Heini Splitter, which were furious when I told him I'm leaving. And a third party was Krakower. But telling these three people, it stands for that the whole of the town knew Paul Kornmehl was leaving, which was dangerous. Am I [? talking ?] too much? Why did you tell them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you tell them?

speaker: interviewee
text: To tell them that they should do exactly the same. Take a risk. So we left on the 24th of November, 1941. We went by train to [NON-ENGLISH]. And from [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, I left with Mellita, you know? And I left with another couple. The couple was Harry Eisland, and her name was Lola Eisland. Then I had a cousin. They also came with us. Her name was Hella, and his name was Hermann Bleisch.

speaker: interviewee
text: The three of us, the three couples of us, left for [NON-ENGLISH], which was about 25 kilometers away from the Belgian border.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you take with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very little, but we looked like refugees. I'll show you later on a photo, and you will see that. I had a long coat on. Don't forget, it was winter. And a hat.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I had with me food. Why food? I don't know, but we needed food. And I didn't have a passport. I think it could have been a few days before we had to put on the star, but I'm not sure about that. The star could have been much later, but there was talk about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: But also worried us enormously was the judenrat. They pointed some very important Jews in Amsterdam to make up a list of all the Jews. And these people thought that it would be in the interest of the Jews to play ball with the Germans. And they made up these lists, and we had to fill in some forms. And we thought by ourself, this is bad news. We met nearly every night at some friend's place, and we discussed it. And we saw this getting darker, and darker, and darker. We were not aware, and I don't think it happened at that time, that Jews were sent to concentration camp. We were not aware of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when we arrived in [NON-ENGLISH], and again, I don't know. We had one tandem. You know what a tandem is? A bicycle, where two were sitting. Then we had another four bikes. We hired them around the corner from the station, and we started biking. How long we biked? Two hours. Three hours. All we knew. We had to get over the front into Belgium. And to our luck-- for some people, it wasn't luck-- we met the [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] are what we call the blue police. They were above the police. We had them later on in the Army, right? There were [NON-ENGLISH], were the toughies in the Dutch police force. And they stopped us there at the road, and they looked at us. And my-- and they said, where are you going? And we said, we are going to the Jewish cemetery. So there were two chances. If they were good, they're going to let us go. If they were bad, they would deliver us to the Germans. And the fellow looked at us. You know, he could see Jewish cemetery. He could see what was in front of him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, don't go straight on. Take the first road to the right. Go straight on the German side there. So it was a good one. So we were lucky. We went to the right. We didn't know the name of a farmer. We just went in to a farmer. And he said to us, we should go to the next one, because his land is close to the Belgium border.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this man, no money involved, told us to go that way, and we should walk about 600 meters. That's where the tram is. The tram way which goes straight to Antwerp. Right?

speaker: interviewee
text: And it took us all together maybe 10, 12 hours, from The Hague to Antwerp. We were lucky, because later on my so very close friend, Norbert Buchsbaum, by the way, I forgot to mention his name. I also warned him, and told him that I'm leaving. So there were four I told the story to, or maybe he got it from Heini Splitter. I'm not sure about it. He got caught on that road. We arrived in Antwerp. We split up. Hella and Hermann, who was later the head of the foreign press in Holland after the war, they went to their sister. Hella had a sister there. I went to my very good friend, the diamond merchant. Diamond merchant was 22 years of age, but he was a diamond-- in diamonds. Joseph Guittler. I mention his name, and his wife was Rosie. And we-- it speaks by itself. We couldn't stay in a hotel because you had to fill in a form. And within hours, the Germans would have been there. And we stayed with the Guittlers. The Eislands, I still don't know, Harry and Lola Eisland, where they stayed. But for us, they were very, very old people, because he was already 29, and I was 21.

speaker: interviewer
text: So looking at him and her, and she were 30, that they were already with one foot in their grave. You know, that everything is relative. We stayed with Joseph for two nights.

speaker: interviewee
text: As a matter of fact, we slept on one bed. And whenever I go to Antwerp, he mentioned that, you know, that we had an affair, that two couples. We couldn't think of anything else. We stayed two nights.

speaker: interviewer
text: And from there we went to Brussels by train. And we stayed in a brothel. And you know why a brothel? Because there's no check up. So when we went to that brothel, we had the address in Antwerp. The mirrors were on the ceiling and on the wall. And full of Germans in the other rooms. You don't have to write down your name.

speaker: interviewee
text: The woman in charge knew that we were Jews, but that address was given to us in Antwerp. And it was-- we were told that it is safe.

speaker: interviewer
text: She told us, if something happens, because sometimes they're checking up the German soldiers in that brothel, you know? Then she will take us-- there were three houses next to each other. And the place is-- the, the square, I remember, is called [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH]. Three houses next to each other. How I remember that? I didn't think about it this morning, but it comes to me now. And we actually had a reasonably good time there. Joseph and Rosie came to visit us, stayed with us three days. We stayed there four days.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then-- I forgot to tell you something. When I arrived in Antwerp, the first thing we did was writing a letter to Switzerland. We had in Switzerland a yeshiva [NON-ENGLISH] by the name of Leizar. We wrote to him, and he forward that letter to Holland. Because there was no communication between Holland and Belgium. Belgium occupied France and France Libre, unoccupied. There was no communication-- so wherever we arrived, from Brussels again, I wrote to Leizar and Leizar sent the letter through. And my name during the war time was Adrian. All during the flight, Adrian.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I always signed with Adrian, right? Adrian. But it was written in such a way because the Germans opened the letters. You know, there was a censor, that one could make out what it is. But my family understood where I was, and that I got out all right.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Brussels, we stayed four days, three or four days. And then we went-- we made arrangement to find the [NON-ENGLISH] in Nancy, which was the occupied France. We went into the train. We are told there will be 12 of us in the train, and we should be dressed down. We shouldn't be dressed up. We were dressed down anyhow. I'll show you later on a photo the way we were dressed. But there were some women out of the 12 which were a little bit dressed up. They were wearing a hat with a feather. But that worried us a little bit. We arrived in the middle of the night, at 2:00 or 3:00 in the morning in Nancy. And we went into a waiting room where there would have been 400 or 500 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: We had to wait for the next train. And we were told the train is at such and such a, a place, right? And when we were sitting there, it was ice cold. It was the end of November.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were sitting there, and with our half asleep, with our coats over, the Germans came in with dogs. And they had a lamp, you know, flashlight. They were shining everybody in the face. We were very young. I had my coat over my head. And Mellita had her coat overhead. I forgot to tell you, the Eislands were with us. Harry and Lola Eisland were with us. Not the Bleisch. They went back to Holland. How they got through exactly, I really don't know. Hella died a few years ago, and Hermann died a year ago, but they got through. He was the head of the foreign press for the last 35, 40 years, and a very close friend of Prince Bernhard. Yes, we were in Nancy there, and fortunately the Germans let us go.

speaker: interviewer
text: We went into the train, and we were told that this is the train for the [FRENCH]. Right? You speak French. Prisoners of war. There was an organization. There were, I think between one and one and a half million [FRENCH] in Germany, working for the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was an organization from Holland to Belgium, to Nancy or another way that they were-- that they were liberated, to go to the free, free France. Most of them then left for Algiers or any of the African colonies. And we are going to have some with us in the train. The train-- and we were told the train would take us so far. And we have to get out on the left-hand side. The [FRENCH] in Nancy was number five. Now it comes to me. And we went to a place called Liesles. I don't know if the French called it Liesles, but it's written that way. L-I-E-S-L-E-S. Liesles. And we had to go out again on the left-hand side. There were 72 prisoners of war, French. And I felt rather pleasant with them, but it was icy cold. It was dark. We were told by the two [FRENCH]. Who we actually paid for these people. We paid money. The [FRENCH] didn't pay anything. That we have to walk very, very slowly. We are not allowed to, to walk. But you know if you walk on ice, and there's 72 people plus the 12 from Antwerp, Brussels, plus the two [FRENCH], even if you walk on your toes it's very noisy. WE walked between five and six hours that night. It was dark. And there was a little river next to us. And we saw the Germans. We had to bend down. And first I thought maybe this is a bit of a show. No, we saw the Germans with their-- with their dogs. The dogs were barking like mad. I thought we never made it. And we had with us a men by the name of Joseph Schmidt-- I have to check this name out-- was a well-known singer from Poland. And when we arrived, when we were over the demarcation line, we were put up in a farm. They gave us red wine, and they gave us whatever we wanted to drink. And that's where the first time I made contact with a drink called [NON-ENGLISH]. We slept. We didn't sleep, because we had some money with us, dollars, not in our pockets, but they were hidden in our clothes. We also had some in our shoes. Not in the shoes, but in the bottom of the shoes. And some also here. And we were not asleep. We were afraid that they're going to rob us, because without money, it was very, very difficult to get through. And then they picked us up. So we were in what you called France Libre, unoccupied France. And we didn't realize that unoccupied France was worse. You know, Vichy France was worse than occupied France. But we are coming to that. We were brought to a place called [FRENCH]. And we were put up in a hotel. We had to go to the police, to the [FRENCH]. And we were told that we have to go to-- again with the Eislands, that we will have to go to a [FRENCH]. That is, they had some [FRENCH] places where the refugees were put up.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I did not want to go there, fortunately. Because all the people of the [FRENCH] within a few months, they were put in the train. And these were the first one, even before the Dutch, before the people in Belgium. They are put in a train to Poland, right? So--

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I forgot to tell you that my sister, Anna Feigenbaum from Antwerp and her husband, and Paulette, when the Germans came to Belgium, they escaped like so many hundreds of thousands to France. And they were in Marseilles. My sister wired this [NON-ENGLISH], we should get out, which we knew ourselves. And we should know what we are doing. And so the first thing, I did in the hotel, [INAUDIBLE] my sister in Marseilles. She couldn't travel.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to change a tape now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon? OK.
